segments.s211-g41: گرازه سر is a compound descriptor: گرازه (young boar) + سر (head) = 'boar‑headed'. گیوگان follows as the substantive being described (followers/men of Giv). با is the preposition 'with' and نهل is a nominal epithet ('youth' / 'sapling' used figuratively here); the whole segment attaches as a single noun phrase meaning something like 'the boar‑headed Giv‑men with Nehal.' Word order is adjective/compound + head + prepositional phrase.
segments.s211-g42: دو is the numeral 'two' modifying گرد. گرد in the given senses list does not show the usual older poetic sense 'warrior/round' here, so it is treated as the counted noun; گرانمایه is an adjective 'esteemed/precious' and شیردل is a compound adjective 'lion‑hearted' that further qualifies the noun. The sequence shows a compressed epithet: 'two (du) گرد — esteemed, lion‑hearted.'
segments.s211-g43: چو at the start functions as a temporal conjunction 'when.' A simple list of proper names follows (رهام، گودرز و فرشیدورد) joined by و; in Persian lists the conjunctions simply link the names and the whole list will be tied to the following predicate.
segments.s211-g44: The second چو again sets a temporal clause 'when.' شیدوش و لهاک are listed as co‑participants; شد is the past of شدن 'to become' (here 'became') and works as the main verb for the clause, while هم‌نبرد is a compound noun meaning 'comrade‑in‑arms' or 'fellow in battle.' Word order: temporal marker + subject list + predicate (شد + complement). Note: some personal names appear here in the text and are serving syntactically as nouns.
segments.s211-g45: ابا is an older/poetic verb meaning 'to refuse' or 'to be unwilling' (here 'refused'). What follows is a list of actors (بیژن گیو) and then کلباد را with را as the definite/object marker marking Kalbad as the (direct) object of the refusing action: 'Bijan and Giv refused Kalbad.'
segments.s211-g46: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that.' بر here has the sense 'against/over,' هم gives the sense 'together/with,' and زدی is the past of زدن 'to strike/throw' — together giving 'who struck (lit. struck together) fire and wind.' آتش و باد را is the object marked by را. The clause therefore explains Kalbad's action: 'who hurled/struck fire and wind at them.'
segments.s211-g47: Again ابا functions as the verb 'refused' or 'was unwilling.' شطرخ نامور is a nominal phrase where نامور 'renowned' modifies شطرخ; گیو را is the object with را marking Giv as definite/object of the refusal. Syntax: verb + noun phrase (epithet) + object marker sequence.
segments.s211-g48: دو is an implied numeral 'two' modifying گرد; گرد here is the counted noun (see note in s21) and گرانمایه 'esteemed/precious' is its adjective. نیو را is another object phrase with را; نیو in the provided senses could be 'ney (reed‑flute)' or 'new,' but here it functions as a name/term in the poem and is followed by the object marker. Word order is numeral + noun + adjective + trailing object phrase.
segments.s211-g9: دل چرخ گردان بدو چاک شد is a nominal phrase + past verb: "دل (heart)" is modified by the compound "چرخ گردان" (the turning/revolving sky; adjective after noun, a common Persian poetic order) and the prepositional pronoun "بدو" (to him/for him). "چاک شد" is a simple past passive/resultative: "was split/rent." Together it reads literally, "the heart of the revolving sky was torn for him."
segments.s211-g10: همه کام خورشید پرخاک شد is a subject + predicate: "همه کام خورشید" means "all the Sun's desires" (همه quantifies کام), followed by the past copula/resultative "پرخاک شد" — literally "became full of dust" (i.e. smothered/covered with dust). Persian places the possessor (خورشید) after the possessed noun (کام) in this poetic line.
segments.s211-g11: چنان شد که کس روی هامون ندید is a clause introduced by چنان شد که ("it became such that / so happened that"). کس is used in the negative sense here ("no one"), and روی هامون is a compound meaning "the face/surface of the plain." ندید is simple past negative (did not see).
segments.s211-g12: ز بس گرد کز رزمگه بردمید is a causal/descriptive adjunct beginning with the poetic preposition ز ("from/because of") plus بس گرد ("so much dust"). کز is the relative/compound form (که + از) linking to رزمگه بردمید: "that from the battlefield rose/carried up." بردمید is a past form meaning roughly "lifted/rose up; carried off."
segments.s211-g13: ببارید الماس از تیره میغ is a short past clause: ببارید (it rained/fell) is an intransitive past; الماس is the plural/collective object ("diamonds/gems") and از تیره میغ is an ablative phrase "from the dark blades" where تیره qualifies میغ. The word order (verb first) is poetic but common in classical Persian.
segments.s211-g14: همی آتش افروخت از گرز و تیغ is another past clause with an emphatic/adverbial همی ("continually / indeed"). آتش (fire) is the subject, افروخت (kindled) is the simple past verb, and از گرز و تیغ is the source phrase "from mace and sword." The coordinating و links the two weapon nouns.
segments.s211-g15: سنانهای رخشان و تیغ سران lists two weapon phrases joined by و: سنانهای رخشان ("gleaming spears") where the adjective رخشان follows and modifies سنانهای, and تیغ سران ("the swords of the chiefs/heads") where سران is a genitive/possessive noun following تیغ. This is a coordinate noun phrase describing bright weapons.
segments.s211-g16: درفش از بر و زیر گرز گران is a nominal clause: درفش (the banner/standard) followed by a locative/relational phrase "از بر و زیر" ("from/at top and bottom" i.e. "above and beneath"), and the noun phrase گرز گران ("heavy maces"). The construction implies the banner was laden or surrounded above and below by heavy maces; Persian often omits an explicit copula in such descriptive lines.
segments.s211-g17: هوا گفتی is an impersonal / idiomatic start meaning 'one would say' or 'it might be said' (literally 'air, you would say'). از ... و از marks material or composition ('of/from ... and of/from ...'). آهنست is a nominal/adjectival predicate (آهن + -ست 'is') meaning 'is of iron.' Word order is topical: 'هوا' (topic) then the comment 'گفتی ... آهنست.'
segments.s211-g18: زمین یکسر از نعل در جوشنست is a verbless (stative) clause describing the ground. یکسر 'entirely/wholly' modifies the following material phrase 'از نعل' ('of horseshoes'). در + جوشنست places the idea inside or as if 'in a cuirass'—literally 'is in a cuirass'—so the whole line means the earth is wholly like armor made of horseshoes. Adjectival predicate (جوشنست) follows the locative/partitive phrase.
segments.s211-g19: چو here introduces a temporal clause 'when'. دریای خون شد is a nominal phrase with شد 'became' as the verb: 'became a sea of blood.' همه modifies the coordinated noun phrase 'دشت و راغ' ('all the plain and ...') to indicate the entirety of those places. Word order is standard: temporal marker + predicate + subject phrase.
segments.s211-g20: This is a simile using چون 'like/as': 'جهان چون شب' = 'the world was like night' and parallel structure 'تیغها چون چراغ' = 'the swords like lamps.' The copula is omitted (poetic zero-copula), so چون + noun supplies the comparison; word order is Subject + comparison.
segments.s211-g21: ز بس is a poetic causal phrase 'because of so much' (ز = 'because of/from', بس = 'much/enough'), followed by ناله کوس 'the lamenting/cry of the drum'. با + کرنای links the instrument/action 'with trumpet(s)' — کرنای here is the blast/cry of the horn. The whole segment gives the cause or reason for the following inability.
segments.s211-g22: همی is an archaic/preverbal element giving durative or emphatic aspect to the verb; here it helps form the past narrative 'could not.' کس ندانست is literal 'no one knew' (negated by the sense of کس = 'no one' plus the negative sense of ندانست 'did not know'). سر را ز پای is an idiomatic expression 'head from foot' (object marked by را and z + noun indicating separation), meaning one could not distinguish up from down.
segments.s211-g23: This is a straightforward clause: سپهبد (the title) + به گودرز 'to Goudarz' (به marks the indirect object) + گفت 'said' followed by آن زمان 'that time' as a temporal adverbial. The verb گفت here introduces the following subordinate clause.
segments.s211-g24: که introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'when/that,' تاریک شد is 'became dark' (شد = became), and گردش آسمان is the subject 'the rotation/circuit of the sky' (گردش = rotation/circuit, آسمان = sky/heavens). Overall: 'at that time when the heavens' turning grew dark.'
segments.s211-g25: Word order is poetic: object 'مرا' (me) appears before the verb. 'گفته بود' is the pluperfect/perfect‑past auxiliary phrasing (had said). 'این ستاره‌شناس' is the subject placed after the verb for poetic inversion. 'را' is the direct‑object marker attached to 'مرا'.
segments.s211-g26: The clause introduced by 'که' ('that') reports the content of what was said. 'امروز' = today; 'تا' here means 'until'; 'شب گذشته' is a fixed phrase meaning 'last night'; 'پاس' refers to a watch/sentry shift. Word order is standard head‑final for the subordinate clause.
segments.s211-g27: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'. 'شمشیر گردان' is a descriptive compound — 'گردان' qualifies 'شمشیر' (whirling/turning swords or sword‑bearing troop), and 'چو' = 'like/as' introduces the simile 'like a black cloud.' The adjective 'سیاه' follows the noun 'ابر' in typical Persian order.
segments.s211-g28: 'همی' is a literary presentive particle indicating continued or habitual action ('keeps/continually'). The verb 'فشاند' (scatters/sprinkles) is in present/habitual form. 'به آوردگاه' is the prepositional phrase 'onto the battlefield' with 'به' = toward/into and 'آوردگاه' = battlefield.
segments.s211-g29: 'سرانجام' means 'in the end' or 'ultimately.' 'ترسم' is the first‑person present of 'to fear' ('I fear'). 'که' introduces the content clause and 'پیروزگر' is the noun 'victor' placed at the end of this segment preparing the following negation.
segments.s211-g30: 'نباشد' is a negative subjunctive/optative form meaning 'there will not be / is not' — used here to express 'there will be no (one)'. 'مگر' = 'except/unless.' 'دشمن' = 'enemy.' 'کینه ور' is a compound meaning 'bearing grudge' or 'vengeful' (here spelled as two words in the segmentation: 'کینه' + 'ور'). The clause therefore says 'there will be no victor except the vengeful enemy.'
segments.s211-g31: 'چو' = 'like/as' begins a list of personal names. The conjunction 'و' links the proper names in the list; names often appear without inflection. Word order is straightforward enumeration of heroes.
segments.s211-g32: 'زره‌دار' is an epithet meaning 'armored' or 'armored warrior' modifying the following names. The remaining items are personal names (here segmented into 'خراد' and 'برزین نیو'); several of these tokens in the supplied sense lists are lexical/semantic readings rather than proper‑name readings, so some sense indexes are not applicable.
segments.s211-g1: This short clause is a nominal phrase in which the participial/adjectival form بیاراست (literally “well‑put/arrayed”) modifies لشکر (host). سپهدار طوس is a genitival apposition: سپهدار (title 'commander') + طوس (proper name) identifies whose host it is. Word order is adjective/verb form → noun → apposition, a common poetic compression that means “the host of Commander Tūs was arrayed.”
segments.s211-g2: This is a list introduced without an explicit verb: بپیلان begins the phrase that enumerates what accompanied the army, followed by three coordinated items joined by و. Grammatically ب- plus a following element functions like a circumstantial ‘with/bringing …’; جنگی, مردان and کوس are coordinated complements describing the army’s equipment and people.
segments.s211-g3: پیاده سوی کوه شد is a compact verbal phrase: پیاده ('on foot') is an adverbial/role word, سوی ('toward') governs the goal کوه ('mountain'), and شد (past of شدن) marks the action in past narrative. با بنه is an instrumental/companial phrase ('with a band/contingent'); بنه is an agentive/collective noun here (poetic use).
segments.s211-g4: This is an appositive locator identifying who was on the right: سپهدار گودرز names the commander (title + name), بر is the preposition 'on/upon', and میمنه is the noun 'right side' giving his position. The whole segment functions as a short displaced clause (‘Goudarz the commander on the right’).
segments.s211-g5: رده برکشیده همه یکسره is a compact verbless (or participial) construction meaning 'the ranks drawn up all together/at once.' رده ('rank') is the head, برکشیده is a past participle/compound indicating 'raised/drawn up', همه modifies quantity ('all'), and یکسره is an adverbial 'completely/at once' modifying the whole phrase.
segments.s211-g6: چو here introduces a comparison/setting ('as/like' or elliptically 'there stood'), followed by two proper names in apposition (رهام, گودرز). بر + میسره parallels the earlier بر میمنه construction to give a lateral position; word order is name(s) → locative preposition → side. میسره is a dialect/poetic locative (here indicating the opposite side), so the clause is an elliptical positional note ('Rohām — Goudarz on the left').
segments.s211-g7: ز introduces the cause ('because of' or 'from'), نالیدن is a verbal noun meaning 'the wailing/laments', and کوس is the instrument (war‑drum). با کرنای adds a coordinated instrumental ('and with the trumpet/horn'), so the whole segment names the sounds causing the effect that follows.
segments.s211-g8: This is the resultant main clause: همی is an emphatic particle ('indeed/just'), آسمان is the subject 'sky/heaven', اندر آمد is the verb phrase 'came in/was moved' (اندر = 'from its place/within'), and ز جای adds an ablative 'from its place'. Together they say the sky itself moved out of its place due to the preceding sounds.
segments.s211-g57: بپیش (prepositional/adverbial) + اندر آمد (compound verb) is a poetic order: 'bepish' = 'forward/toward' comes before the verb; 'اندر آمد' = 'came in/forward'. The subject 'سپهدار طوس' follows the verb, a common inversion in epic Persian.
segments.s211-g58: This is a verb phrase where the omitted subject (referring back to طوس) performs the action. 'پیاده' names the kind of force (infantry/foot soldiers) and precedes the past-tense verb 'بیاورد' ('he brought'), then items are listed with و.
segments.s211-g59: The verb برکشیدند is third-person plural past 'they drew/raised' and takes 'صفی' (a row/line) as its object; 'پیش سوار' is a locative phrase 'in front of the horsemen'. Word order (object + verb + locative) is typical of elevated/poetic Persian.
segments.s211-g60: A simple coordinating noun phrase enumerating who/what is present: 'سپردار و ژوپین‌ور و نیزه‌دار' — nouns joined by و. These items are in apposition to the earlier 'سوار' (the cavalry/horsemen) or further specify the troops.
segments.s211-g61: 'مجنبید' is a negative imperative ('do not move/ do not stir'). 'گفت' follows as the verb of saying (introducing reported speech), while 'ایچ از جای خویش' is the negation phrase 'not at all from his/your place' with the possessive suffix ش on خوی (خویش). The order places the imperative before the speech‑tag, a poetic inversion.
segments.s211-g62: This is the second clause of the reported command: 'سپر با سنان اندرارید پیش'. 'اندرارید' here is an imperative (plural) 'bring/place/put forward'; the direct objects 'سپر' and 'سنان' are linked by با ('with/along with'), and 'پیش' marks the forward/ in‑front position.
segments.s211-g63: 'ببینیم' is a hortatory/first‑person‑plural present tense 'let us see'. 'تا' introduces the subordinate clause ('that / so that / how'); 'این نبرده سران' is a noun phrase where 'نبرده' functions as a negative participial adjective ('not‑taken' / 'unconquered') modifying 'سران' ('leaders').
segments.s211-g64: 'چگونه' opens the interrogative subordinate clause ('how'). 'گزارند' is third‑person plural present used here as 'do/perform/wield' (how they handle), and 'گرز گران' ('mace(s) heavy') is the object with the adjective following the noun, a normal Persian order.
segments.s211-g49: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the following proper names. The sequence of names is simply coordinated with و 'and' between them; Persian lists names in the normal (noun then conjunction) order. Each name is a nominative/subjective item that sets the scene for the main clause that follows.
segments.s211-g50: This is the main predicative clause: نبد (negative past copula) + هیچ پیدا (a collocation meaning 'no sign/appearance') followed by the coordinated objects درنگ و فسوس 'pause and whispering/hesitation'. نبد is an archaic/poetic negative form equivalent to نبود 'there was not', so the clause reads 'there was no ...'.
segments.s211-g51: چنین گفت هومان is a fixed narrative phrase 'thus said Hūmān' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت used here as the reported-speech verb 'said', and هومان the speaker). که introduces a content clause (that-clause) whose content follows; امروز کار 'today the matter' is the topic of that clause.
segments.s211-g52: نباید introduces a necessity/ prohibition ('it should not / must not be') and takes a subordinate clause marked by که. چون دی بود is a comparative/time phrase 'as (it was) yesterday' (چون = 'as/like', دی = 'yesterday', بود = past copula 'was'), and کارزار is the noun 'battle' serving as the standard of comparison or the reference event: 'should not be like yesterday's battle.'
segments.s211-g53: همه جان شیرین بکف برنهید is an imperative/hortative instruction. همه 'all/everyone' modifies جان شیرین 'dear lives' (a poetic epithet), بکف ('into the hand' or 'into my hand' — an elided possessor is understood from context) and برنهید is an imperative plural form meaning 'set/put (upon)/place'. The verb is placed at the end as in normal Persian.
segments.s211-g54: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/as'. من is the subject 'I'. برخروشم is a deverbal/nominal phrase 'my uprising in anger' (برخروش + possessive -م), and دمید is a past-tense verb 'blows / breathes / is blown' used here in a poetic sense of 'my fury being kindled/issued'. The coordinating و links to دهید, an imperative/plural present 'you give' (or 'you shall give'), so the clause means 'when my wrath is kindled, I blow and you give.'
segments.s211-g55: تهی کرد باید ازیشان زمین is a verbal phrase with modal ضرورت 'باید' ('must'): تهی کرد ('make empty/clear') is the verb phrase, ازیشان ('of/from them') is the prepositional object, and زمین ('the land') is the direct object/locative affected by the action. Word order here places the result/state (تهی) first (poetic inversion), then the verb and necessity marker.
segments.s211-g56: نباید که آیند is another prohibition construction 'they must not come', with که introducing the subordinate clause. زین پس is the temporal adverbial 'from now on / henceforth' (زین + پس), and بکین is a poetic verbal form meaning 'to take vengeance' (here infinitival/intentional). The whole segment reads 'they must not come henceforth to take (vengeance).'
segments.s211-g33: ز is the poetic form of از and here means 'from/out of'. The phrase ز صف در میان سپاه places two prepositional phrases in order: 'out of the ranks' (ز صف) and 'into the midst of the host' (در میان سپاه). آمدند is simple past (3rd person plural) used narratively: 'they came.'
segments.s211-g34: جگر خسته is a figurative noun+adjective phrase: جگر (seat of feeling/heart) + خسته (weary) = 'weary at heart' or 'with exhausted hearts.' The coordinating و links a second descriptive noun کینه‌خواه ('vengeful' / 'avenger') to the same plural verb آمدند; the repetition of آمدند emphasizes the arrival. آمدند is again past plural.
segments.s211-g35: بابر is a proper name (subject). اندر آمد is an archaic/pre-classical locative+verb pattern meaning 'entered/appeared.' ز هر سو is a circumstantial phrase 'from every side,' where سو is 'side.' غریو (placed at the end) names the sound/cry that arose. آمد is simple past (3rd sing.).
segments.s211-g36: بسان introduces a simile 'like/as.' شب تار here is a poetic collocation meaning 'dark night' (تار used poetically); انبوه دیو is a noun phrase 'a dense/numerous throng of demons.' The whole segment is descriptive and links back to غریو in the previous segment; the verb is omitted here because this is a simile modifying the cry.
segments.s211-g37: وزان is a linking adverb 'and/from that (moment)/thereupon.' روی here functions adverbially as 'on/at that turn' (archaic use). هومان بکردار is the name plus epithet 'Homan, of good/steady conduct.' کوه is placed before the verb to form a simile with the following verb phrase: 'an army like a mountain' (کوه modifies لشکر that follows).
segments.s211-g38: بیاورد is the narrative past (3rd person; classical form) 'he brought/caused to come.' لشکر is the direct object 'an army/host.' همه همگروه is a descriptive postmodifier: همه 'all' + همگروه 'fellow-members/comrades' = 'all comrades together' or 'a host made up entirely of comrades.'
segments.s211-g39: وزان پس is a temporal/continuative link 'and from that / thereafter, then.' گزیدند is past plural 'they chose.' مردان مرد is a tautological/epithetic construction (plural + singular noun) meaning 'men of men' — an emphatic formula for 'the foremost men' or 'heroes.'
segments.s211-g40: که introduces a purpose/result clause here, 'so that/in order that.' بردشت is 'victory' (object of the clause). سازند is present-subjunctive (3rd plural) used after که for purpose: 'that they may make/achieve.' جای نبرد is a genitival phrase 'the place of battle' = 'the battlefield'; word order literal is 'place (of) battle.' Together: 'so that they might bring victory to the battlefield' (i.e., win the battle).

sentences.s211-s21: The boar‑headed Giv‑men with Nehal — two esteemed, lion‑hearted warriors.
sentences.s211-s22: When Roham, Goudarz, and Farshidvord — and when Shidush and Lahak — became comrades in battle.
sentences.s211-s23: Bijan and Giv refused Kalbad — he who struck fire and wind at them.
sentences.s211-s24: Abā Shatrakh the famed refused Giv — two esteemed Niu (object marked).
sentences.s211-s5: The heart of the revolving sky was rent for him; all the Sun's desires became covered with dust.
sentences.s211-s6: It became such that no one saw the face of the plain, from so much dust that rose from the battlefield.
sentences.s211-s7: Diamonds rained from the dark blades; fire continually kindled from maces and swords.
sentences.s211-s8: Gleaming spears and the swords of the chiefs — the standard, above and below, was heavy with maces.
sentences.s211-s9: One might say the air is of maces and of iron; the earth is everywhere like a cuirass of horseshoes.
sentences.s211-s10: When the plains and wastes became a sea of blood, the world was like night and the swords like lamps.
sentences.s211-s11: Because of so many laments of drum and trumpet-blast, no one could tell head from foot.
sentences.s211-s12: The spahbed said to Goudarz at that time when the rotation of the heavens darkened.
sentences.s211-s13: This astrologer had told me that from today until last night there were three watches.
sentences.s211-s14: From the whirling swords, like a black cloud, blood keeps being scattered onto the battlefield.
sentences.s211-s15: In the end I fear that the victor will be none other than the vengeful enemy.
sentences.s211-s16: Like Shidush and Roham and Gostaham and Giv — the armored Kharrad and Barzin Niu.
sentences.s211-s1: Commander Tūs's host was arrayed, with elephants, fighters, men and war‑drums.
sentences.s211-s2: The foot‑soldiers moved toward the mountain with a detachment; Commander Goudarz on the right.
sentences.s211-s3: All the ranks were drawn up at once; Rohām — Goudarz on the left.
sentences.s211-s4: Because of the lamenting of the drums and the blasts of the trumpets, the heavens themselves rose from their place.
sentences.s211-s29: The commander Tūs came forward and brought foot soldiers, elephants, and war‑drums.
sentences.s211-s30: They drew up a line before the cavalry — the commander, the jupon‑clad, and the spearmen.
sentences.s211-s31: He said, 'Do not move at all from your place; set shield and spear before you.'
sentences.s211-s32: Let us see how these unconquered leaders bear the heavy maces.
sentences.s211-s25: When Goudarz, Pīrān, Hūmān and Tūs appeared, there was not the slightest pause or whisper.
sentences.s211-s26: Hūmān said thus: 'Today the business must not be like yesterday's battle.'
sentences.s211-s27: Lay all your dear lives into my hand; when my anger rises, I will blow forth, and you shall give.
sentences.s211-s28: He must clear the land of them; they must not come hereafter to take (vengeance).
sentences.s211-s17: They came out of the ranks into the midst of the host; they came with weary hearts and bent on vengeance.
sentences.s211-s18: Babr entered among them; from every side rose a cry, like a dark night and a throng of demons.
sentences.s211-s19: Thereupon Homan, steadfast in conduct, brought forth an army like a mountain, all comrades together.
sentences.s211-s20: Thereafter they chose men of men so that they might win victory on the field of battle.
